model_narrative: Optimization model 'dicegrid_mip' (metadata auto-synthesized from the model by build_optichat_queries.py — no curated constraint-gen seed exists). Sets: f, dice. Decision variables: wnx, fval, comp. Objective: maximize 'Maximize the common number of pairwise wins'.

vars.name: wnx
vars.doc: Number of wins (common per die)

vars.name: fval
vars.doc: Face value on each die (may be fractional in the design model)

vars.name: comp
vars.doc: comp[d,f,fp]=1 implies face f of die d beats face fp of the next die

objective.expr_var: Maximize the common number of pairwise wins

constraints.name: eq1
constraints.intent: Count the wins per die

constraints.name: eq3
constraints.intent: Definition of the non-transitive (cyclic) winning relation

constraints.name: eq4
constraints.intent: Distinct, strictly increasing face values per die

query: We're looking at a packaging-friendly variant where the three dice can't all carry big numbers up top. Suppose we require the combined total of the highest face across all three dice to stay no greater than 35 pips. What per-die win count does the optimizer return once the top faces have to share that budget?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_top_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval[d, 'face6'] for d in model.dice) <= 35)

query: There's a proposal on the table to stop any single die from hogging the high numbers. The idea is to hold the third die's total pip count to no more than thirty percent of the pip total summed across all three dice. With the third die limited to that share of the overall pips, what win count does the design land on?
expected_pyomo: model.share_d3 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval['dice3', f] for f in model.f) <= 0.30 * sum(model.fval[d, f] for d in model.dice for f in model.f))

query: We're exploring a more legible engraving rule that applies to every die: as you read the faces from smallest to largest on any die, each face has to jump by at least three pips over the face just below it on that same die. With that wider spacing enforced across all three dice, what does the optimizer report for the per-die win count?

query: We're weighing a two-part design spec as a single package: first, the combined total of the highest face across the three dice can't exceed 38 pips, and at the same time the combined total of the lowest face across the three dice has to be at least 14 pips. So the set gets squeezed from the top and lifted from the bottom together. Where does the per-die win count come out under both rules at once?
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_tops = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval[d, 'face6'] for d in model.dice) <= 38)
model.floor_lows = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval[d, 'face1'] for d in model.dice) >= 14)

query: Die1 leans way too hard on its three middle faces to rack up matchup wins against the next die — that feels like the design is over-relying on a narrow band. Shouldn't those three middle faces of die1, taken together, be allowed to win no more than three of the head-to-head face comparisons against die2? Force that cap on the middle band's wins and show me what the model is protecting by letting them carry so much.
expected_pyomo: model.why_mid_band = Constraint(expr=sum(model.comp['dice1', f, fp] for f in ['face2', 'face3', 'face4'] for fp in model.f) <= 3)

query: Die1 comes back looking weak — its six faces barely add up to anything compared with where they could sit in the 1-to-18 range. Shouldn't die1 be made to pull its weight, with its six face values summing to at least 80 pips overall instead of loafing in the low end? Force that aggregate floor on die1 and tell me what the model is giving up to keep that die so light.
expected_pyomo: model.why_mass_d1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval['dice1', f] for f in model.f) >= 80)

query: It bugs me that die2's biggest face is allowed to tower over the middle of die3 — that seems like it's letting die2 run away at die3's expense. Wouldn't it be fairer to insist that die2's highest face sit no higher than the value on die3's third-lowest face, so die2 can't outstrip die3's mid-range? Tie those two together and tell me what's keeping the model from accepting that ceiling.
